new_constraint_classes.label: Non-Deception Constraint
new_constraint_classes.definition: A prohibition on engaging in deceptive acts in professional practice, including misrepresenting one's qualifications or technical expertise or presenting inaccurate content in a professional deliverable.
text_references: Fundamental Canon I.5 requires an Engineer to “avoid deceptive acts,” which was not violated here.
Engineer A did not misrepresent their qualifications or technical expertise, nor did the AI-generated text contain inaccuracies.
new_constraint_classes.source_text: Further, the use of AI to assist with writing does not inherently constitute deception. Engineer A did not misrepresent their qualifications or technical expertise, nor did the AI-generated text contain inaccuracies. Fundamental Canon I.5 requires an Engineer to “avoid deceptive acts,” which was not violated here.

match_decision.reasoning: The discussion invokes Canon I.5's prohibition on deceptive acts, which is exactly the existing Non-Deception Constraint already in the ontology from prior extraction; no new class is needed.

new_constraint_classes.label: Personal Preparation Seal Constraint
new_constraint_classes.definition: A prohibition on affixing a professional signature or seal to any plan or document dealing with subject matter in which the engineer lacks competence, or to any plan or document not prepared under the engineer's direction and control.
text_references: Code section II.2.b states that, “[e]ngineers shall not affix their signatures to any plans or documents dealing with subject matter in which they lack competence, nor to any plan or document not prepared under their direction and control.”
new_constraint_classes.source_text: Code section II.2.b states that, “[e]ngineers shall not affix their signatures to any plans or documents dealing with subject matter in which they lack competence, nor to any plan or document not prepared under their direction and control.” By relying on AI-assisted tools without a comprehensive verification process of its output, Engineer A risked violating this requirement.

match_decision.reasoning: The Code II.2.b shall-not-affix-signatures prohibition is the same boundary as the already-recorded Personal Preparation Seal Constraint; the discussion applies it to the AI-generated design documents.

new_constraint_classes.label: Confidentiality Constraint
new_constraint_classes.definition: A prohibition on disclosing or exposing information a professional holds in confidence for a client, including placing client-provided information into open-source systems where unauthorized parties may access it, without the client's prior consent.
text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
When Engineer A uploaded Client W’s information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client’s private information in the public domain.
new_constraint_classes.source_text: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client. While careful review and checking of AI-generated content was consistent for ethical use, this does not end the inquiry into Engineer A’s actions. When Engineer A uploaded Client W’s information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client’s private information in the public domain.

match_decision.reasoning: The Code II.1.c limit on sharing confidential client information without prior consent is the same boundary as the Confidentiality Constraint already extracted from the facts pass; the discussion supplies the source provision.

new_constraint_classes.label: Tool Substitution Prohibition Constraint
new_constraint_classes.definition: A prohibition on using technology as a replacement or substitute for the engineer's own engineering judgment, including blindly accepting tool-generated recommendations without considering, challenging, and understanding the resulting outputs.
text_references: technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement
Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood.
new_constraint_classes.source_text: The BER notes that in BER Case 98-3 , the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement. Much like directing an engineering intern to solve a problem, responsible use of AI requires an engineer to outline solution guidelines and constraints. Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood.

match_decision.reasoning: The must-not-substitute-technology-for-judgment boundary the Board applies to Engineer A is the same concept as the already-recorded Tool Substitution Prohibition Constraint.

text_references: Fundamental Canon I.5 requires an Engineer to “avoid deceptive acts,” which was not violated here.
constraint_individuals.source_text: Further, the use of AI to assist with writing does not inherently constitute deception. Engineer A did not misrepresent their qualifications or technical expertise, nor did the AI-generated text contain inaccuracies. Fundamental Canon I.5 requires an Engineer to “avoid deceptive acts,” which was not violated here.

match_decision.reasoning: New case individual recording the Canon I.5 boundary as it holds on Engineer A in this case.

constraint_individuals.constraint_statement: Engineer A must not engage in deceptive acts, including misrepresenting their qualifications or technical expertise or presenting inaccurate content in the professional report.
constraint_individuals.applicability_condition: When preparing and delivering professional work products for Client W, including a report drafted with AI language processing software.
constraint_individuals.temporal_scope: Throughout Engineer A's professional practice on the engagement
constraint_individuals.case_context: The Board held that using AI to assist with writing does not inherently constitute deception and found that Engineer A did not violate this boundary, since qualifications were not misrepresented and the AI-generated text contained no inaccuracies.

text_references: Code section II.2.b states that, “[e]ngineers shall not affix their signatures to any plans or documents dealing with subject matter in which they lack competence, nor to any plan or document not prepared under their direction and control.”
By relying on AI-assisted tools without a comprehensive verification process of its output, Engineer A risked violating this requirement.
constraint_individuals.source_text: Code section II.2.b states that, “[e]ngineers shall not affix their signatures to any plans or documents dealing with subject matter in which they lack competence, nor to any plan or document not prepared under their direction and control.” By relying on AI-assisted tools without a comprehensive verification process of its output, Engineer A risked violating this requirement.

match_decision.reasoning: New case individual recording the II.2.b signature boundary as it holds on Engineer A for the AI-generated deliverables.

constraint_individuals.constraint_statement: Engineer A must not affix their signature to any plan or document dealing with subject matter in which they lack competence, nor to any plan or document not prepared under their direction and control.
constraint_individuals.applicability_condition: When signing or sealing the report and the AI-generated design documents for Client W, particularly where AI-assisted tools produced the content without a comprehensive verification process of the output.
constraint_individuals.temporal_scope: At the time of signing and sealing the deliverables
constraint_individuals.case_context: The Board found the report satisfied direction and control because of Engineer A's thorough review, but held that relying on the AI-assisted drafting tool with only a high-level review of the design documents risked violating this requirement.

constraint_individuals.identifier: Engineer A Client Consent Sharing Boundary
text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
When Engineer A uploaded Client W’s information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client’s private information in the public domain.
constraint_individuals.source_text: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client. While careful review and checking of AI-generated content was consistent for ethical use, this does not end the inquiry into Engineer A’s actions. When Engineer A uploaded Client W’s information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client’s private information in the public domain. The facts here do not indicate Engineer A obtained permission from Client W to use the private information in the public domain.

match_decision.reasoning: New case individual recording the II.1.c confidentiality boundary as it holds on Engineer A regarding Client W's information.

constraint_individuals.constraint_statement: Engineer A must not share Client W's confidential information without the prior consent of Client W, including uploading it into an open-source AI interface where it is tantamount to placing it in the public domain.
constraint_individuals.applicability_condition: When handling information gathered in confidence from Client W during the engagement, including any use of that information in open-source AI systems.
constraint_individuals.temporal_scope: For the duration of the engagement and while the information remains confidential
constraint_individuals.case_context: The Board found that uploading Client W's information into the AI open-source interface placed the client's private information in the public domain, and the facts do not indicate Engineer A obtained Client W's permission.

text_references: technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement
Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood.
These are steps that, in this case, Engineer A chose not to follow.
constraint_individuals.source_text: The BER notes that in BER Case 98-3 , the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement. Much like directing an engineering intern to solve a problem, responsible use of AI requires an engineer to outline solution guidelines and constraints. Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood. Only after the engineer in Responsible Charge has satisfied themselves that the proposed solution is in accordance with their own and professional standards should the design/report be accepted. These are steps that, in this case, Engineer A chose not to follow.

match_decision.reasoning: New case individual recording the technology-substitution boundary the Board applies to Engineer A's use of AI tools in this case.

constraint_individuals.constraint_statement: Engineer A must not use AI technology to replace or substitute for their own engineering judgment, and must not blindly accept AI-generated recommendations without considering and challenging them and understanding the resulting outputs.
constraint_individuals.applicability_condition: When using the AI language processing software and the new-to-market AI-assisted drafting tool to produce the report and design documents while in responsible charge of the project.
constraint_individuals.source: BER Case 98-3, as applied by the Board
constraint_individuals.temporal_scope: Throughout the preparation and acceptance of the AI-generated report and design documents
constraint_individuals.case_context: The Board applied the BER Case 98-3 doctrine that technology must not substitute for engineering judgment, noting that a design or report should be accepted only after the engineer in responsible charge is satisfied it meets professional standards, and found these are steps Engineer A chose not to follow.
